questions.question: Which CSS filter function applies a Gaussian blur to an element?
questions.explanation: The `blur()` filter function applies a Gaussian blur to the element. It accepts a radius value in pixels — a larger value produces a more blurred result.

questions.question: What does `filter: grayscale(100%)` do to an element?
choices: Converts the element to completely black and white
Reduces the element's brightness to zero
Inverts all the colors of the element
questions.explanation: The `grayscale(100%)` filter fully desaturates the element, converting it to black and white. A value of 0% leaves the element unchanged.

questions.question: Which filter function shifts all colors of an element by a specified angle on the color wheel?
questions.explanation: The `hue-rotate()` function rotates the hue of all colors in the element by the specified angle (in degrees) on the color wheel. For example, `hue-rotate(180deg)` shifts all colors to their complementary hues.

questions.question: How does `filter: invert(100%)` affect an element's appearance?
choices: Reverses all colors so that white becomes black and vice versa
Removes all color, leaving only transparency
questions.explanation: The `invert(100%)` filter completely inverts the colors of the element, making each color its opposite on the RGB spectrum (white becomes black, red becomes cyan, etc.).

questions.question: The `filter: ___()` function gives an element a warm, brownish-yellow tone similar to an old photograph.
questions.explanation: The `sepia()` filter function applies a sepia tone to the element, simulating the warm coloring of vintage photographs. A value of 100% is fully sepia.

questions.question: The `filter: ___()` function creates a shadow effect that conforms to the shape of an image, including transparent areas.
questions.explanation: Unlike `box-shadow`, which creates a rectangular shadow, `drop-shadow()` generates a shadow that follows the alpha channel (transparency) of the element, respecting its actual shape.
